items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs don't change with output (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change directly with output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs never change at all, ever.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are paid once and variable costs are paid daily.
items.why: Fixed costs are fixed WITH RESPECT TO OUTPUT — they can change for other reasons (rent goes up). The distinction is whether producing more or less changes the cost.

items.q: What causes the supply curve to shift?
items.correct: Changes in production costs, technology, taxes/subsidies, number of producers, expectations — anything affecting supply other than the good's own price.
items.misconception: When demand rises, the supply curve shifts right to meet it.
items.random_wrong: Supply shifts only when consumers want more.
items.why: Rising demand causes a movement along the supply curve, not a shift of it. Supply shifts only when something on the supply side changes. Students entangle the two curves.

items.q: What defines a pure public good?
items.correct: Non-rivalry (one person's use doesn't reduce others') and non-excludability (you can't stop people benefiting) — e.g. national defence, street lighting.
items.misconception: A public good is anything provided by the government.
items.random_wrong: A public good is anything that's free.
items.why: Government provides many private goods (e.g. NHS treatment is rival). The economic definition is about the GOOD's properties, not who provides it. Students confuse provider with technical category.

items.q: Which is NOT a factor of production?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Money. “Land” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's The whole economy. “The price of bread” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many factors of production are there?
items.why: The real figure is 4 factors. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
